page: UpCloud CLOUDNATIVE-16xCPU-192GB is a memory-optimized virtual server hosted on the KVM hypervisor. It features 16 shared vCPUs mapped to 16 physical cores of an AMD EPYC 7542 processor running at 2.0 GHz. The system is equipped with 192.0 GB of RAM, yielding a high memory-to-core ratio of 12.0 GB per core. This configuration does not include any bundled local storage or complimentary public IPv4 addresses, and it lacks dedicated GPU hardware.

Performance benchmarks indicate a clear tradeoff between raw single-core compute and multi-threaded memory operations. The shared CPU allocation results in poor single-core performance, as evidenced by a stress-ng single-core score in the bottom 10 percent. However, multi-core benchmarks, such as the stress-ng multi-core test and Geekbench multi-core score, fall into the average tier. Memory bandwidth tests show stable read and write speeds, though the Passmark memory score is relatively weak, placing in the bottom 25 percent.

From a cost-efficiency perspective, this server offers high memory density on a shared-resource model, making it a viable option for memory-intensive workloads that do not require continuous, dedicated single-core CPU performance. Appropriate workloads include large-scale caching layers, database operations, multi-threaded text and image processing, and static web serving, where the 192.0 GB RAM capacity can be fully leveraged.
description: UpCloud CLOUDNATIVE-16xCPU-192GB is a memory-optimized virtual server running on the KVM hypervisor. Powered by an AMD EPYC 7542 processor, it provides 16 shared vCPUs and 192.0 GB of RAM, translating to 12.0 GB of memory per core. The server does not include bundled local storage or a GPU. Benchmark data reveals a performance profile characterized by poor single-core CPU execution but average multi-core and database performance. This resource distribution makes the server highly cost-efficient for memory-heavy applications that can tolerate shared CPU allocation. It is well-suited for database operations, caching, and multi-threaded processing workloads.
